title: CFR Cluj

infobox.value: * *Ceferiștii* (The [CFR](/wiki/C%C4%83ile_Ferate_Rom%C3%A2ne "Căile Ferate Române") People) * *Clujenii* (The [Cluj](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca") People) * *Feroviarii* (The Railwaymen) * *Alb-vișinii* (The White and Burgundies) * *Echipa din Gruia* (The Team from [Gruia](/wiki/Gruia,_Cluj-Napoca "Gruia, Cluj-Napoca")) * *Campioana Provinciei* (The Provincial Champion)
10 November 1907; 118 years ago (10 November 1907) (as *Kolozsvári Vasutas Sport Club*)

(Romanian pronunciation: [[t͡ʃefeˌre ˈkluʒ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian") or [[ˌt͡ʃefere ˈkluʒ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")) or simply **CFR**, is a Romanian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the city of [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca"), [Cluj County](/wiki/Cluj_County "Cluj County"), that competes in the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"). Founded as *Kolozsvári Vasutas Sport Club* in 1907, when [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") was part of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), the club's current name is an [acronym](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym") for *[Căile Ferate Române](/wiki/C%C4%83ile_Ferate_Rom%C3%A2ne "Căile Ferate Române")* ("Romanian Railways").

**Fotbal Club CFR 1907 Cluj**, commonly known as **CFR Cluj** (Romanian pronunciation: [[t͡ʃefeˌre ˈkluʒ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian") or [[ˌt͡ʃefere ˈkluʒ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")) or simply **CFR**, is a Romanian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the city of [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca"), [Cluj County](/wiki/Cluj_County "Cluj County"), that competes in the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"). Founded as *Kolozsvári Vasutas Sport Club* in 1907, when [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania") was part of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), the club's current name is an [acronym](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym") for *[Căile Ferate Române](/wiki/C%C4%83ile_Ferate_Rom%C3%A2ne "Căile Ferate Române")* ("Romanian Railways").

Before its promotion to Liga I in [2004](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Divizia_A "2004–05 Divizia A"), CFR Cluj primarily competed in the [lower divisions](/wiki/Romanian_football_league_system "Romanian football league system"). Since then, the club has increasingly relied on [foreign players](/wiki/List_of_foreign_Liga_I_players "List of foreign Liga I players") to achieve success. In the [2005–06 season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Divizia_A "2005–06 Divizia A"), CFR participated in its first [European competition](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"), the [Intertoto Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "UEFA Intertoto Cup"), finishing as runners-up. With substantial financial backing from former owner Árpád Pászkány, the club won its first national championship in the [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Liga_I "2007–08 Liga I"), ending a 17-year dominance by [capital](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest")-based teams.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Between [2017](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Liga_I "2017–18 Liga I") and [2022](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Liga_I "2021–22 Liga I"), "the White and Burgundies" won five consecutive [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") championships. The club has secured 17 domestic trophies, all in the 21st century: eight Liga I titles, five [Cupa României](/wiki/Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Cupa României"), and four [Supercupa României](/wiki/Supercupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Supercupa României"). In addition to becoming a prominent force in [Romanian football](/wiki/Football_in_Romania "Football in Romania"), CFR qualified three times each for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") and [Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") group stages, and twice for the [Europa Conference League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_Conference_League "UEFA Europa Conference League") group stages.

CFR shares a fierce rivalry with local club [Universitatea Cluj](/wiki/FC_Universitatea_Cluj "FC Universitatea Cluj"), with their matches known as *[Derbiul Clujului](/wiki/Cluj_derby "Cluj derby")*. In recent years, the club has also developed less intense rivalries, notably with [FCSB](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB"), as they frequently compete for the Liga I title.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Establishment and early years (1907–1969)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Establishment and early years (1907–1969)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Kolozsv%C3%A1ri_Vasutas_Sport_Club_1911.jpg/330px-Kolozsv%C3%A1ri_Vasutas_Sport_Club_1911.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kolozsv%C3%A1ri_Vasutas_Sport_Club_1911.jpg)

*Kolozsvári Vasutas Sport Club* team in 1911.

CFR was founded in 1907, when the city of [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca") (then *Kolozsvár*) was part of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary"), under the name *Kolozsvári Vasutas Sport Club* ("Kolozsvár Railway Sports Club"). From 1907 to 1910, the team played in the municipal championship. However, the club did not have any notable achievements during this time. In 1911, the team won the newly organized Championship of Transylvania. The club consistently finished in second place in that competition between 1911 and 1914, a competition that was interrupted because of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). After the war, Transylvania joined Romania and the club accordingly changed its name to **CFR Cluj**, maintaining its links with the national rail organisation, this time the [Romanian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") state railway carrier, [Căile Ferate Române](/wiki/C%C4%83ile_Ferate_Rom%C3%A2ne "Căile Ferate Române"), hence the acronym. They went on to win two regional titles, in 1918–19 and 1919–20.

Between 1920 and 1934 the club did not have any notable achievements. Between 1934 and 1936, CFR played for two seasons in the [Divizia B](/wiki/Divizia_B "Divizia B"), ranking sixth in the [1934–35 season](/wiki/1934%E2%80%9335_Divizia_B "1934–35 Divizia B") and eighth in the [1935–36 season](/wiki/1935%E2%80%9336_Divizia_B "1935–36 Divizia B"). In 1936, CFR was relegated to the [Divizia C](/wiki/Divizia_C "Divizia C"), where the team played for two seasons, finishing [second](/wiki/1936%E2%80%9337_Divizia_C "1936–37 Divizia C") and 4th, respectively. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), CFR played for one season in the Divizia C, earning the promotion to the Divizia B. Before the start of the [1947–48 season](/wiki/1947%E2%80%9348_Divizia_B "1947–48 Divizia B"), the team merged with another local club, [Ferar Cluj](/wiki/Ferar_Cluj "Ferar Cluj"), and played in the [Divizia A](/wiki/Divizia_A "Divizia A") for the [very first time](/wiki/1947%E2%80%9348_Divizia_A "1947–48 Divizia A") in history. Unfortunately, the team [lasted only two years in the first league](/wiki/1948%E2%80%9349_Divizia_A "1948–49 Divizia A") and would not play there again for another 20 years. In 1960, another merger, this time with [Rapid Cluj](/wiki/Dermata_Cluj "Dermata Cluj") resulted in *CSM Cluj*. In 1964, the team's name was changed to *Clujeana*. In that same year, the club's junior team won the national championship. Three years later, the team's name was reversed yet again to **CFR Cluj**.

### Return to the top flight (1969–1976)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Return to the top flight (1969–1976)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/CFR_Cluj_League_Performance.svg/330px-CFR_Cluj_League_Performance.svg.png)](/wiki/File:CFR_Cluj_League_Performance.svg)

Chart depicting the yearly positions of CFR in the national leagues between 1943 and 2017.

In [1969](/wiki/1968%E2%80%9369_Divizia_B "1968–69 Divizia B"), CFR finished first in Divizia B with 40 points, five more than their rival, [Politehnica Timișoara](/wiki/FC_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara "FC Politehnica Timișoara"). The conclusive game of that season was a 1–1 draw with Politehnica. Politehnica had a 1–0 lead at half-time, but CFR came back with a fine header.

During the summer of 1969, CFR Cluj advanced to Divizia A under the leadership of coach [Constantin Rădulescu](/wiki/Constantin_R%C4%83dulescu_(footballer,_born_1924) "Constantin Rădulescu (footballer, born 1924)"). Rădulescu was originally from southern Romania, but he grew to manhood in the atmosphere of [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"). Before coaching, he had played for CFR and another well-known local team, [Universitatea Cluj](/wiki/CFM_Universitatea_Cluj "CFM Universitatea Cluj") (or *U Cluj*), during the 1940s. In the [1969–70 first league championship](/wiki/1969%E2%80%9370_Divizia_A "1969–70 Divizia A"), CFR made its debut with a 2–0 victory over [ASA Târgu Mureș](/wiki/ASA_T%C3%A2rgu_Mure%C8%99_(1962) "ASA Târgu Mureș (1962)"). The next few games did not go as well; although there was a 1–0 win to [Politehnica Iași](/wiki/FC_Politehnica_Ia%C8%99i_(1945) "FC Politehnica Iași (1945)"), there were 2 losses to [Steaua București](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București") (1–3) and [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") (0–2). These and other defeats were a factor in the team's supposed downhill slide. However, the following spring CFR bounced back with a win over ASA Târgu Mureș (1–0), after a goal from [Octavian Ionescu](/wiki/Octavian_Ionescu_(footballer,_born_1949) "Octavian Ionescu (footballer, born 1949)"), and averted relegation.

At the beginning of CFR's [second season in Divizia A](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_Divizia_A "1970–71 Divizia A"), Rădulescu was replaced by [Eugen Iordache](/wiki/Eugen_Iordache "Eugen Iordache") as head coach. During his tenure, CFR did not do well, and Rădulescu was swiftly brought back. Even so, CFR Cluj found itself again at the bottom of the table before the winter break. The spring of 1971 was somewhat better, although CFR struggled again to avoid relegation. CFR's last game of that season, against [UTA Arad](/wiki/FCM_UTA_Arad "FCM UTA Arad"), was a memorable one. CFR led 1–0 at half-time. UTA Arad, however, overturned the match after scoring twice. Nonetheless, the persistence of the players from Cluj was rewarded with a late goal, tying the game at 2–2. UTA went on to [play in the European Cups](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_European_Cup "1970–71 European Cup"), but, most importantly, CFR avoided relegation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/4f/Constantin_R%C4%83dulescu.png)](/wiki/File:Constantin_R%C4%83dulescu.png)

[Constantin Rădulescu](/wiki/Constantin_R%C4%83dulescu_(footballer,_born_1924) "Constantin Rădulescu (footballer, born 1924)"), the pivotal coach of the club during the 1970s.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

The [1971–72 season](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_Divizia_A "1971–72 Divizia A") started off badly for CFR. Losses to Dinamo București (1–3); [Crișul Oradea](/wiki/FC_Bihor_Oradea_(1958) "FC Bihor Oradea (1958)") (0–1, after a last-minute penalty kick), and [Jiul Petroșani](/wiki/CS_Jiul_Petro%C8%99ani "CS Jiul Petroșani") (1–2 after two regrettable own-goals) meant CFR's demise after the first round of the championship – the team finished at the bottom, with only seven points. CFR's return was dramatic, although inconsistent at times. The team won some important games, such as a 1–0 with [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/CS_Universitatea_Craiova "CS Universitatea Craiova") and a 3–0 with [Petrolul Ploiești](/wiki/FC_Petrolul_Ploie%C8%99ti "FC Petrolul Ploiești"). By the end of the season, however, CFR was again struggling to stay in Divizia A. CFR was tied at half-time after having led with 2–0 in their game against [Politehnica Iași](/wiki/FC_Politehnica_Ia%C8%99i_(1945) "FC Politehnica Iași (1945)"). In the second half, two late goals from Ionescu and Petrescu saved the team from relegation. When Rădulescu and his players got back home to Cluj, 3,000 fans turned out to celebrate their performance.

During the summer of 1972, CFR made an important transfer. [Mihai Adam](/wiki/Mihai_Adam "Mihai Adam"), from Universitatea Cluj, was traded for Soos. Adam had been twice Romania's top scorer, and was considered one of the best Romanian players of his generation. He and the rest of the team would make the [1972–73 season](/wiki/1972%E2%80%9373_Divizia_A "1972–73 Divizia A") the most successful in CFR's history. The team achieved its highest ranking ever in Romanian football, fifth in Divizia A. Several important results concluded a great season, including a 2–0 victory against [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"), a 2–2 draw against [Sportul Studențesc București](/wiki/FC_Sportul_Studen%C8%9Besc_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Sportul Studențesc București"), and another draw, 1–1, with Steaua București. Additionally, the stadium that CFR continues to use even today was built in 1973. To celebrate the completion of the stadium, CFR Cluj played a friendly game against [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba_national_football_team "Cuba national football team"). The game ended in a 2–1 victory for CFR.

The [1973–74 season](/wiki/1973%E2%80%9374_Divizia_A "1973–74 Divizia A") was a rather bad one for CFR, as it barely saved itself from relegation, ranking 14th at the end of the season. The only notable achievement of that season was Mihai Adam's third title as Romania's top goal-scorer who, even though he was 33 years old, scored 23 goals. The [1974–75 season](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Divizia_A "1974–75 Divizia A") was much like the one before: CFR struggled to avoid relegation, achieving its objectives all the while. The [1975–76 season](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_Divizia_A "1975–76 Divizia A") marked CFR's relegation and its last season in Divizia A during the 20th century. A contributing negative factor was the age of the team, with most of its players in their 30s.

### Lower leagues (1976–2002)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Lower leagues (1976–2002)")]

During the [1977–78](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_Divizia_B "1977–78 Divizia B") season, CFR attempted to make a comeback. However, the team finished only second in [Divizia B](/wiki/Divizia_B "Divizia B"), after [Baia Mare](/wiki/FC_Baia_Mare "FC Baia Mare"). Four years later, [CFR slid further down](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_Divizia_B "1981–82 Divizia B"), into the third division, [Divizia C](/wiki/Divizia_C "Divizia C"). From then on, the team would alternate between the second and third leagues. In the [1982–83](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_Divizia_B "1982–83 Divizia B") season, CFR won Series X of Divizia C and promoted to Divizia B under its longstanding coach, Dr. Constantin Rădulescu, with a squad that included Paniță, Fereștean, Oltean, H. Popa, Olariu, Toth, A. Mureșan, Tuță, Jucău, Pop, Rău, Furnea, O. Mureșan, Țegean, Tatu, Sipoș, Rus, I. Popa, Csudom, and Berindei.[[8]](#cite_note-9) In the 1990s, CFR struggled financially and found itself more than once on the brink of bankruptcy. Nevertheless, several very talented players were raised, including [Cristian Dulca](/wiki/Cristian_Dulca "Cristian Dulca"), [Attila Piroska](/wiki/Attila_Piroska "Attila Piroska"), [Cristian Coroian](/wiki/Cristian_Coroian "Cristian Coroian"), and [Alin Minteuan](/wiki/Alin_Minteuan "Alin Minteuan").

### Pászkány takeover and first domestic titles (2002–2012)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pászkány takeover and first domestic titles (2002–2012)")]

In January 2002, a new sponsor, Árpád Pászkány, head of S.C. ECOMAX M.G., founded a new commercial sport society, with ECOMAX M.G. as the primary shareholder. By the end of the 2001–02 season in [Divizia C](/wiki/Liga_III "Liga III"), CFR had been promoted back to Divizia B (later on Liga II).

The [summer of 2003](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Divizia_B "2002–03 Divizia B") was very important for CFR as many new talented players were transferred including Cătălin Bozdog, [Adrian Anca](/wiki/Adrian_Anca "Adrian Anca"), [Cristian Turcu](/wiki/Cristian_Turcu "Cristian Turcu"), and Sabin Pîglișan. With these players and others, CFR entered the first league after [a successful season in Divizia B](/wiki/2003%E2%80%9304_Divizia_B "2003–04 Divizia B"). CFR began the season strongly, holding first place for a while. Then the club's main sponsor, Árpád Pászkány, became involved in a public scandal during which Pászkány accused several referees of corruption. The affair plagued the team and resulted in the dismissal of head coach [George Ciorceri](/wiki/George_Ciorceri "George Ciorceri").

CFR lost several consecutive games before the scandal subsided. After the winter break, Cioceri was replaced by [Aurel Șunda](/wiki/Aurel_%C8%98unda "Aurel Șunda"). In the spring of 2004, Sunda's team had a nearly perfect run, winning 14 out of 15 games, with only one draw. One round before the season's end, CFR was in second place, one point behind the [Jiul Petroșani](/wiki/CS_Jiul_Petro%C8%99ani "CS Jiul Petroșani") in first. But when Jiul was held to a draw by [Gaz Metan Mediaș](/wiki/CS_Gaz_Metan_Media%C8%99 "CS Gaz Metan Mediaș"), and CFR won their last match 3–0, CFR advanced to the top of the league for the first time in 28 years. In the summer of 2004, CFR acquired many new valuable Romanian players, including [Vasile Jula](/wiki/Vasile_Jula "Vasile Jula") and [Radu Marginean](/wiki/Radu_Marginean "Radu Marginean").

CFR Cluj's [first year back in Divizia A](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Divizia_A "2004–05 Divizia A") was strong, yet inconsistent. CFR finished sixth after the first half of the 2004–05 championship. It was during this time that CFR played one of its most popular games ever, defeating [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") at home. The final score was 4–2, after two goals each by Adrian Anca and Sorin Oncică. However, the second half of the championship proved disappointing for CFR, as it gathered only 12 points after 15 games. The team finished 11th, avoiding relegation.

The summer of 2005 brought significant change to CFR Cluj. The club's executives signed the team up for the [UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "UEFA Intertoto Cup"), being CFR's first European adventure. CFR began well, qualifying for the second round after two victories against [FK Vetra](/wiki/FK_Vetra "FK Vetra") (3–2 and 4–1).

Also, the Romanian international [Dorinel Munteanu](/wiki/Dorinel_Munteanu "Dorinel Munteanu") came to CFR from [Steaua București](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București"). Munteanu would have the dual role of player-coach. His first game produced one of CFR's greatest successes. CFR defeated [Athletic Bilbao](/wiki/Athletic_Bilbao "Athletic Bilbao") of Spain 1–0 (although almost all players from Bilbao's side were from the reserve squad) during the second round of the [2005 UEFA Intertoto Cup](/wiki/2005_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "2005 UEFA Intertoto Cup"). The only goal of the match was scored by [Cosmin Tilincă](/wiki/Cosmin_Tilinc%C4%83 "Cosmin Tilincă") with a header. CFR then lost in [Bilbao](/wiki/Bilbao "Bilbao") (1–0) but still qualified to the next round after a penalty shootout.

Munteanu's team played the next game at Cluj, against French club [Saint-Étienne](/wiki/AS_Saint-%C3%89tienne "AS Saint-Étienne"). Adrian Anca played one of the greatest games in his career, even though the match ended in a 1–1 draw. Anca hit the crossbar with a header early in the game, and Tilincă pushed the ball into the net from the rebound. Anca then went on to earn a penalty, but did not score. He then hit the crossbar a second time in the second half. The away game, in France, was also an eventful game for CFR Cluj. The game began well for CFR, as [Cristian Coroian](/wiki/Cristian_Coroian "Cristian Coroian") scored from a penalty kick, earned by Adrian Anca. The second half went less smoothly for CFR; [Julien Sablé](/wiki/Julien_Sabl%C3%A9 "Julien Sablé") scored for Saint-Étienne, tying the game at 1–1. This was followed by CFR player [László Balint](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Balint "László Balint")'s elimination. However, a [Cosmin Tilincă](/wiki/Cosmin_Tilinc%C4%83 "Cosmin Tilincă") goal gave the team the ability to tie with the French at the last minute. The game ended in a 2–2 draw, so CFR went on to the next qualifying stage due to its away goals. In the next round CFR easily disposed of [Zalgiris Vilnius](/wiki/Zalgiris_Vilnius "Zalgiris Vilnius"), 2–1 in Lithuania and 5–1 at home.

For the final match of the 2005 UEFA Intertoto Cup, CFR Cluj's opponent was another French franchise, [RC Lens](/wiki/RC_Lens "RC Lens"). The first game, at Cluj, ended in a 1–1 draw with both sides having scored from free kicks. Cristian Turcu scored for CFR. The second game was played at [Lens](/wiki/Lens,_Pas-de-Calais "Lens, Pas-de-Calais") in front of 30,000 French fans. The Romanian players showed signs of exhaustion and conceded three goals. Player-coach Dorinel Munteanu scored a goal from a free kick in the 89th minute. Thus ended CFR Cluj's Intertoto journey. CFR then finished fifth at the end of the [2005–06 domestic season](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Divizia_A "2005–06 Divizia A"). During the [2006–07 season](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Liga_I "2006–07 Liga I"), major changes at the club started to occur. Dorinel Munteanu resigned as player-coach, and was replaced by [Cristiano Bergodi](/wiki/Cristiano_Bergodi "Cristiano Bergodi"). Foreign players from Western Europe and South America were transferred. A partnership with Portuguese club [Benfica](/wiki/S.L._Benfica "S.L. Benfica") was signed. On 22 July 2007, CFR Cluj celebrated its centenary year by playing a friendly game against Benfica and inaugurating the new illumination system at its stadium.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Chelsea_%282%29_v_%281%29_CFR_Cluj.jpg/330px-Chelsea_%282%29_v_%281%29_CFR_Cluj.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chelsea_(2)_v_(1)_CFR_Cluj.jpg)

CFR Cluj's players lining up at [Stamford Bridge](/wiki/Stamford_Bridge_(stadium) "Stamford Bridge (stadium)") before a duel with [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C."), December 2008.

The team's new coach, Romanian [Ioan Andone](/wiki/Ioan_Andone "Ioan Andone"), formerly of [Omonia Nicosia](/wiki/AC_Omonia "AC Omonia") and [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București"), started the [2007–08 season](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Liga_I "2007–08 Liga I") well, with CFR Cluj leading the league by eight points halfway through the season and remaining undefeated. Their form was not as good in the second half of the season, and they were overtaken by [Steaua București](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București") with two games remaining. Even though Steaua crushed [Gloria Buzau](/wiki/FC_Gloria_Buz%C4%83u "FC Gloria Buzău") 5–0 in the last matchday, it was not enough to bring the title to Ghencea, since CFR won the derby against [Universitatea Cluj](/wiki/CFM_Universitatea_Cluj "CFM Universitatea Cluj") and won the title, becoming the first team outside [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") to win the title in nearly two decades.[[9]](#cite_note-10) Three days later, CFR Cluj completed a league and cup [double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)"), beating [Unirea Urziceni](/wiki/FC_Unirea_Urziceni "FC Unirea Urziceni") in the [Romanian Cup final](/wiki/2008_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei_Final "2008 Cupa României Final").

By winning the league, CFR Cluj qualified for the group stage of the [2008–09 UEFA Champions League season](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League_2008%E2%80%9309 "UEFA Champions League 2008–09"). They were drawn in Group A against [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") of England, [A.S. Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma") of Italy, and [Bordeaux](/wiki/FC_Girondins_de_Bordeaux "FC Girondins de Bordeaux") of France and given little chance of progressing, with odds of 300–1 being given on them winning the competition.[[10]](#cite_note-11) In their opening game, CFR caused a shock by beating Roma in the [Italian capital](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), 2–1,[[11]](#cite_note-12) with Argentine [Juan Culio](/wiki/Emmanuel_Culio "Emmanuel Culio") scoring the brace. Expectations were further exceeded by holding the previous season's finalists, Chelsea, to a 0–0 draw.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

The end of the [2008–09](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Liga_I "2008–09 Liga I") season saw CFR finish fourth; the team had two coaching staff changes in the second part of the competition and did not manage to secure a second title. The Romanian Cup was kept for a consecutive year at Cluj, and thus they played against Unirea Urziceni (the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") champions that season) in the Supercupa României. CFR became the first club not from Bucharest to claim the trophy in [2009](/wiki/2009_Supercupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2009 Supercupa României").

In the [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Liga_I "2009–10 Liga I") season, the team won the league title for the second time in its history, exhibiting the heavy investments in the club's infrastructure, management, and squad transfers. Managed by coach [Andrea Mandorlini](/wiki/Andrea_Mandorlini "Andrea Mandorlini"), CFR Cluj also kept the Romanian Cup and qualified for the UEFA Champions League group stage. As a premier, the 2009–2010 CFR Fans' Trophy was awarded to [Cristian Panin](/wiki/Cristian_Panin "Cristian Panin") as voted by supporters and football reviewers. The trophy is to be awarded every year by the CFR Cluj fans associations to the player that receives the highest aggregate number of votes online and highest per match rating respectively.[[13]](#cite_note-14) The 2010–11 CFR Fans' Trophy was awarded to captain [Ricardo Cadú](/wiki/Cad%C3%BA_(footballer,_born_1981) "Cadú (footballer, born 1981)") and the 2011–2012 CFR Fans' Trophy was awarded to goalkeeper [Beto Pimparel](/wiki/Beto_(footballer,_born_1982) "Beto (footballer, born 1982)").

The [2011–12](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Liga_I "2011–12 Liga I") season brought the league title to Cluj for the third time. Starting under Jorge Costa's supervision, the team maintained a spot in the top three. After a few major defeats close to the end of the season, Costa was replaced by Ioan Andone. Under Andone, CFR won all the remaining matches except for one draw, and finished first. Later that year, [FC Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") defeated CFR Cluj in the [Romanian Supercup](/wiki/Romanian_Supercup "Romanian Supercup") with 6–4 after penalties, handing them their first defeat in a final.

### Financial difficulties and bounceback (2012–2017)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Financial difficulties and bounceback (2012–2017)")]

After 2012, poor management saw the club go through a sharp decline, finishing 9th in the [2012–13](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_Liga_I "2012–13 Liga I") season, though in the Champions League they performed admirably, finishing 3rd on goal difference in a group with [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United "Manchester United"), [Galatasaray](/wiki/Galatasaray "Galatasaray") and [SC Braga](/wiki/SC_Braga "SC Braga") with 10 points, a record still standing for a Romanian team. Their Champions League campaign culminated in a 1–0 away win at Old Trafford against Manchester United, with a long shot from [Luis Alberto](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Alberto_(footballer,_born_1983) "Luís Alberto (footballer, born 1983)"). They were drawn against [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan") in Europa League, where they were eliminated 5–0 on aggregate.

CFR had a quiet 2013–14 season, finishing 5th and earning a Europa League berth, mainly due to the fact that 4th placed Dinamo filed for insolvency and thus were ineligible for European competitions. During this time owner Pászkány faced legal charges and neglected the team, which lead to serious financial difficulties that would culminate in the following seasons.

CFR Cluj began the [2014–15](/wiki/2014%E2%80%9315_Liga_I "2014–15 Liga I") season well, but financial difficulties led to [insolvency](/wiki/Insolvency "Insolvency") which subsequently started a period of poor performances.[[14]](#cite_note-15) After failing to fully remunerate five former club players, the [Romanian Football Federation](/wiki/Romanian_Football_Federation "Romanian Football Federation") decided to deduct 24 points from CFR, which placed them in the last position in Liga I. Many players left the club as a result, and *Ceferiștii* challenged the Federation's decision to the [Court of Arbitration for Sport](/wiki/Court_of_Arbitration_for_Sport "Court of Arbitration for Sport"). In May 2015 the Court ruled in their favour, restoring the deducted points, which helped the team secure a third-place finish in the league championship. CFR Cluj won the [2016 Cupa României final](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2015–16 Cupa României") played against [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") after [penalty shootouts](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)"),[[15]](#cite_note-16) being their first trophy since 2012.

### Domestic dominance and return to European competitions (2017–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Domestic dominance and return to European competitions (2017–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Dan_Petrescu_%28cropped%29_-_Chelsea_Legends_1_Inter_Forever_4_%2827457025407%29.jpg/250px-Dan_Petrescu_%28cropped%29_-_Chelsea_Legends_1_Inter_Forever_4_%2827457025407%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dan_Petrescu_(cropped)_-_Chelsea_Legends_1_Inter_Forever_4_(27457025407).jpg)

[Dan Petrescu](/wiki/Dan_Petrescu "Dan Petrescu") led the club to the 2017–18, 2019–20, and 2021–22 league titles. He partially managed them in the 2018–19 and 2020–21 Liga I seasons as well, which CFR also won.

In early 2017, businessman Marian Băgăcean purchased 62% stake in the club in early 2017.[[2]](#cite_note-Băgăcean-2) After finishing the [2016–17 Liga I](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Liga_I "2016–17 Liga I") campaign on the fourth place the same year on 30 May, CFR Cluj finally got out of insolvency and could participate in European competitions starting with the 2018–19 season again.[[16]](#cite_note-17) In June 2017, [Dan Petrescu](/wiki/Dan_Petrescu "Dan Petrescu") replaced [Vasile Miriuță](/wiki/Vasile_Miriu%C8%9B%C4%83 "Vasile Miriuță") as the head coach of the team, with the goal of a European cup return and an ambitious transfer campaign to support it.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

On 20 May 2018, "the Railwaymen" won 1–0 over defending champions [Viitorul Constanța](/wiki/FC_Viitorul_Constan%C8%9Ba "FC Viitorul Constanța") and clinched [their fourth Liga I title](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Liga_I "2017–18 Liga I") as they finished one point above [FCSB](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București") in the table.[[18]](#cite_note-19) CFR also came victorious in the subsequent [2018 Supercupa României](/wiki/2018_Supercupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2018 Supercupa României") played against [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/CS_Universitatea_Craiova "CS Universitatea Craiova"), this time under the management of coach [Edward Iordănescu](/wiki/Edward_Iord%C4%83nescu "Edward Iordănescu").[[19]](#cite_note-20) However, Iordănescu was replaced after just three games and [Toni Conceição](/wiki/Toni_Concei%C3%A7%C3%A3o "Toni Conceição") was brought back for his third term as a manager. The club's European campaign was cut short after Luxembourgish side [F91 Dudelange](/wiki/F91_Dudelange "F91 Dudelange") won the [UEFA Europa League play-off round](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_UEFA_Europa_League_qualifying_phase_and_play-off_round#Play-off_round "2018–19 UEFA Europa League qualifying phase and play-off round") 5–2 on aggregate; due to Dudelange's [underdog](/wiki/Underdog "Underdog") status, daily newspaper *[Gazeta Sporturilor](/wiki/Gazeta_Sporturilor "Gazeta Sporturilor")* regarded CFR's elimination as "the biggest shame in the history of Romanian football".[[20]](#cite_note-21)

In May 2019, again under the management of Dan Petrescu, "The Railwaymen" earned [their fifth Liga I title](/wiki/2018%E2%80%9319_Liga_I "2018–19 Liga I"). Unlike the previous year, the club had a fruitful run in European competitions. After getting past [Astana](/wiki/FC_Astana "FC Astana") and [Maccabi Tel Aviv](/wiki/Maccabi_Tel_Aviv_F.C. "Maccabi Tel Aviv F.C."), CFR Cluj defeated Scottish team [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_F.C "Celtic F.C") in the [Champions League third qualifying round](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Champions_League#Third_qualifying_round "2019–20 UEFA Champions League"). In the [play-off](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Champions_League_qualifying_phase_and_play-off_round#Play-off_round "2019–20 UEFA Champions League qualifying phase and play-off round") they met [Slavia Prague](/wiki/SK_Slavia_Prague "SK Slavia Prague"), but lost both matches 0–1 and were sent to the [Europa League group stages](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Europa_League#Group_stage "2019–20 UEFA Europa League"). There, CFR were drawn against [Lazio](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio"), [Rennes](/wiki/Stade_Rennais_F.C. "Stade Rennais F.C.") and again Celtic. They finished second behind the latter and earned a total of twelve points in the group, a Romanian record in European competitions. In the [round of 32](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Europa_League#Round_of_32 "2019–20 UEFA Europa League"), CFR was eliminated by [Sevilla](/wiki/Sevilla_F.C. "Sevilla F.C.") on the [away goals rule](/wiki/Away_goals_rule "Away goals rule") after two draws—the Spaniards went on to win the [final](/wiki/2020_UEFA_Europa_League_Final "2020 UEFA Europa League Final") 3–2 against [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan"), on 21 August 2020. On 3 August, CFR Cluj won the third consecutive title and sixth overall, after a final fixture win over rivals [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/CS_Universitatea_Craiova "CS Universitatea Craiova").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

CFR started the [2020–21 UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_UEFA_Champions_League "2020–21 UEFA Champions League") season by beating Maltese side [Floriana](/wiki/Floriana_F.C. "Floriana F.C."). They were then eliminated by Croatian side [Dinamo Zagreb](/wiki/GNK_Dinamo_Zagreb "GNK Dinamo Zagreb") at home in a [penalty shoot-out](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)"). Dropping down to the [Europa League](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_UEFA_Europa_League "2020–21 UEFA Europa League"), they made it to the group stage after defeating Nordic sides [Djurgårdens IF](/wiki/Djurg%C3%A5rdens_IF_Fotboll "Djurgårdens IF Fotboll") and [Kuopion Palloseura](/wiki/Kuopion_Palloseura "Kuopion Palloseura"). Drawn with [AS Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma"), [BSC Young Boys](/wiki/BSC_Young_Boys "BSC Young Boys"), and [CSKA Sofia](/wiki/PFC_CSKA_Sofia "PFC CSKA Sofia") in Group A, they eventually finished third and were eliminated from the competition from the group stage.[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24) During late 2020, Edward Iordănescu became once again coach of the club after the departure of Dan Petrescu. On 18 May 2021, Iordănescu Jr. managed to win the league title of the [2020–21 Liga I](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Liga_I "2020–21 Liga I") season. This was Iordănescu Jr.'s first national title as head coach. In addition, winning the title with CFR thereby allowed the club to play the final of the [2021 Supercupa României](/wiki/2021_Supercupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2021 Supercupa României") (i.e. the Romanian supercup) against [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/CS_Universitatea_Craiova "CS Universitatea Craiova"), the winners of the [2020–21 Cupa României](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "2020–21 Cupa României"), which they eventually lost after 2–4 on penalty shoot-out.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Following the end of the season Iordănescu left the club and was replaced by [Marius Șumudică](/wiki/Marius_%C8%98umudic%C4%83 "Marius Șumudică"). The latter failed to qualify the club for the group stage of either the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_UEFA_Champions_League "2021–22 UEFA Champions League") or [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_UEFA_Europa_League "2021–22 UEFA Europa League"), being consequently dismissed and replaced by the returning [Dan Petrescu](/wiki/Dan_Petrescu "Dan Petrescu"). Under Petrescu, CFR qualified for the group stage of the inaugural [UEFA Europa Conference League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_Conference_League "UEFA Europa Conference League"), competing in Group D with Dutch side [Alkmaar](/wiki/AZ_Alkmaar "AZ Alkmaar"), Czech side [Jablonec](/wiki/FK_Jablonec "FK Jablonec"), and Danish side [Randers](/wiki/Randers_FC "Randers FC").[[25]](#cite_note-26) The club debuted with an away 1–0 loss at [Jablonec nad Nisou](/wiki/Jablonec_nad_Nisou "Jablonec nad Nisou") in the Czech Republic against [FK Jablonec](/wiki/FK_Jablonec "FK Jablonec") on 16 September 2021 and consequently on the 4th place in the group after the first fixture.[[26]](#cite_note-27) In the second fixture however, the club managed to draw 1–1 with Randers FC and thereby gained its first point in the Europa Conference League.[[27]](#cite_note-28) After their away match with Randers, which they lost 1–2, CFR were mathematically eliminated from advancing to the Round of 16 and finished in fourth place in Group D of the inaugural UEFA Europa Conference League season. This outcome remained unchanged despite their eventual 2–0 victory in the last home match against Jablonec, which earned them their first three points in the competition, bringing their total to four.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/CFR_Cluj%2C_campioana_Rom%C3%A2niei_pentru_al_cincilea_sezon_consecutiv_%2852082964438%29.jpg/330px-CFR_Cluj%2C_campioana_Rom%C3%A2niei_pentru_al_cincilea_sezon_consecutiv_%2852082964438%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:CFR_Cluj,_campioana_Rom%C3%A2niei_pentru_al_cincilea_sezon_consecutiv_(52082964438).jpg)

CFR Cluj celebrating their fifth consecutive Liga I title at the end of the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Liga_I "2021–22 Liga I").

While CFR Cluj's European campaign in the 2021–22 season proved to be quite disappointing, the team had an almost flawless domestic season. They secured the top spot in the regular season by accumulating 76 out of 90 possible points, finishing 16 points ahead of the second-place team. During the play-offs, CFR experienced their worst run of form throughout the season, suffering two consecutive defeats against Universitatea Craiova and FCSB, which narrowed the gap between CFR and the latter to just two points. However, CFR swiftly recovered from their setback and embarked on a remarkable winning streak, securing four consecutive victories, including an impressive 6–0 win over FC Argeș. On 14 May, with FCSB's draw against Voluntari, CFR only needed a win in their match against Craiova to clinch the league title, which they accomplished with a 2–1 win. With this triumph, CFR became the team with the most titles won outside the capital (eight) and the only Romanian team in the 21st century to claim five consecutive titles.

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Stadium")]

Main article: [Dr. Constantin Rădulescu Stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Dr._Constantin_R%C4%83dulescu "Stadionul Dr. Constantin Rădulescu")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Dr_Constantin_Radulescu_Stadium_%288122856688%29.jpg/330px-Dr_Constantin_Radulescu_Stadium_%288122856688%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dr_Constantin_Radulescu_Stadium_(8122856688).jpg)

The [Dr. Constantin Rădulescu Stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Dr._Constantin_R%C4%83dulescu "Stadionul Dr. Constantin Rădulescu") in 2012

CFR Cluj played its home games on the [Stadionul Orășenesc Cluj](/wiki/Stadionul_Or%C4%83%C8%99enesc_Cluj "Stadionul Orășenesc Cluj") from 1911 - when it was inaugurated - until 1960, when it was demolished, to make way to [Stadionul Ion Moina](/wiki/Stadionul_Ion_Moina "Stadionul Ion Moina") back then named *Stadionul Municipal*. On "Municipal", CFR played from 1960 until 1973.

CFR Cluj presently plays nearly all of its home games at the Dr. Constantin Rădulescu Stadium, which was expanded in 2008 to seat a maximum capacity of 23,500.[[1]](#cite_note-stadium-1) It meets all of UEFA's regulations and can also host [Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") matches. In 2006–07, with an investment of €30 million, the club upgraded the field with higher quality turf, built a state of the art [lighting system](/wiki/Floodlights_(sport) "Floodlights (sport)"), and updated its infrastructure. All the work was completed for the club's 100th anniversary in 2007, when a friendly game was played against Portuguese side [Benfica](/wiki/S.L._Benfica "S.L. Benfica").

## Support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Support")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Galeria1.jpg/250px-Galeria1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Galeria1.jpg)

CFR Cluj supporters on 14 August 2006 at a domestic match against [FC Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") in Romania

A 2011 survey has shown that CFR Cluj has the fourth-largest number of supporters in Romania.[[28]](#cite_note-29) They have many fans in Cluj-Napoca, but also in some other parts of the country. Since the 2014 withdraw of important groups such as "Patriots" and "Commando Gruia", the fans have a single big group called "Peluza Vișinie", which consists of former members of older groups such as "Romaniacs", "Juvenes", "Gruppo Gara", "Valacchi", "Pride 1907", "Nostra Famiglia", and "1907". There is another group of supporters which consists of ethnic Hungarians who currently sit in the Tribuna 1 sector of the stadium. Their group is named KVSK, which is the Hungarian name of CFR. They had such major conflicts with the Romanians ultras group 'Peluza Vișinie' and decided to go to matches alone. Their support is less vocal and visible, but they are a consistent part of the active fans.

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Main article: [Cluj derby](/wiki/Cluj_derby "Cluj derby")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Picture050resizegl1.jpg/250px-Picture050resizegl1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Picture050resizegl1.jpg)

CFR fans at a home game in 2010

CFR Cluj has a fierce rivalry with their local opponents [Universitatea Cluj](/wiki/FC_Universitatea_Cluj "FC Universitatea Cluj").[[29]](#cite_note-30) According to journalist Răzvan Toma, the first match between the two teams was played on 13 October 1920, when CFR thrashed Universitatea 8–0 on a field based in the [Central Park](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca_Central_Park "Cluj-Napoca Central Park").[[30]](#cite_note-Razvan_Toma-31) History and statistics website Romanian Soccer regards a 1–3 loss by CFR (which had just merged with [Ferar Cluj](/wiki/Ferar_Cluj "Ferar Cluj") on 7 December 1947) as the first Liga I meeting between the two teams.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

In 2019, [Liga Profesionistă de Fotbal](/wiki/Liga_Profesionist%C4%83_de_Fotbal "Liga Profesionistă de Fotbal")'s website referred to a match between [FCSB](/wiki/FCSB "FCSB")—formerly FC Steaua București—and CFR Cluj as "[the Romanian Derby](/wiki/Eternal_derby_(Romania) "Eternal derby (Romania)")",[[32]](#cite_note-Romanian_Derby-33) a name generally used for the meetings between the former club and their cross-town rivals [Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București"). This stems from the fact that after the 2000s CFR and FCSB were often some of the main contenders for the national title, and during the late 2010s the rivalry exacerbated further as Dinamo lost its power status. As of 2023, CFR and FCSB have met each other over 70 times in the first division.

*Ceferiștii* also hold milder rivalries with Dinamo București, [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"), [Universitatea Craiova](/wiki/CS_Universitatea_Craiova "CS Universitatea Craiova"), and [Politehnica Timișoara](/wiki/FC_Politehnica_Timi%C8%99oara "FC Politehnica Timișoara").

### Other records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CFR_Cluj&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Other records")]

* Since the [2012–13 season](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_UEFA_Champions_League_group_stage "2012–13 UEFA Champions League group stage"), CFR Cluj holds the record for the most points obtained by any Romanian club in the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") group stages, with **10** points, having recorded 3 wins, 1 draw, and 2 losses.
* CFR Cluj also holds the record for most points scored by any Romanian club in the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") group stages, with **12** points, having recorded 4 wins and 2 losses in the [2019–20 season](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_UEFA_Europa_League "2019–20 UEFA Europa League")
